industries.name: Permanent Magnets (NdFeB)
uses: Praseodymium substitutes for neodymium in NdFeB permanent magnets — PrFeB has comparable magnetic properties to NdFeB and Pr-Nd mixed metal (didymium) is used interchangeably with pure neodymium in many magnet grades
Didymium (Nd-Pr alloy) from rare earth separation is used directly in magnet production without further separation — economically advantageous since Nd-Pr separation is costly and often unnecessary
Pr addition to NdFeB magnets improves corrosion resistance and can enhance magnetic properties in certain temperature ranges — some premium magnet grades specify minimum praseodymium content
Growing demand for NdFeB magnets in EV traction motors, wind turbine generators, and consumer electronics drives proportional demand for praseodymium alongside neodymium

companies.name: Shin-Etsu Chemical
companies.use: World's largest NdFeB magnet producer. Consumes significant praseodymium (as didymium alloy with neodymium) in magnet production. Supplies high-performance NdFeB magnets for EV motors, wind turbines, and consumer electronics.

companies.name: TDK Corporation
companies.use: Major NdFeB magnet manufacturer using Nd-Pr mixed metal in its NEOREC and NEODELTA magnet grades for automotive, industrial, and consumer electronics applications.

industries.name: Glass Coloring & Ceramics
uses: Praseodymium oxide (Pr₆O₁₁) produces a bright, clean yellow-green color in ceramic glazes and glass — used in ceramic tile colorants (praseodymium yellow, Pr-Zr yellow) for high-temperature stable yellow glazes on floor and wall tiles
Pr-doped zirconium silicate (Pr:ZrSiO₄) yellow ceramic pigment: one of the most widely used inorganic yellow pigments in ceramic tiles — stable to 1300°C, lightfast, and chemically inert

Praseodymium in welder's goggles glass: didymium glass (Nd+Pr) absorbs the intense yellow sodium D-line emission (589nm) from glass-blowing flames — standard protective filter for glassblowers and lampworkers
Praseodymium-doped fiber amplifiers (Pr-PDFA) for 1300nm optical fiber amplification — largely superseded by erbium-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFA) at 1550nm in modern telecom systems

companies.name: Ferro Corporation (Prince International)
companies.use: Major ceramic pigment manufacturer producing praseodymium yellow (Pr:ZrSiO₄) and other rare earth-based inorganic pigments for the ceramic tile industry. Supplies yellow, green, and specialty pigments to tile manufacturers in Spain, Italy, China, and Brazil.

companies.name: Endeka Ceramics
companies.use: Major European ceramic pigment producer making Pr-Zr yellow, green, and specialty rare earth pigments for Spanish, Italian, and global ceramic tile manufacturers.

industries.name: Specialty Alloys & Catalysts
uses: Praseodymium in misch metal (Ce-La-Pr-Nd alloy) used as pyrophoric lighter flint material — the original commercial use of rare earths (Carl Auer von Welsbach, 1903). Misch metal flints spark when struck, used in cigarette lighters and survival strikers

Praseodymium alloyed with magnesium: Mg-Pr alloys (Elektron 21, WE54) for lightweight aerospace castings requiring elevated temperature strength — used in helicopter gearboxes, aircraft seat frames, and racing car gearbox housings
Praseodymium oxide as catalyst promoter in three-way automotive catalysts — similar role to cerium, providing supplementary oxygen storage and improving catalyst efficiency
Praseodymium cobalt (PrCo₅) permanent magnets for high-temperature applications above the range of NdFeB — used in high-temperature sensors and actuators

companies.name: Magnesium Elektron (Luxfer Holdings)
companies.use: Specialist magnesium alloy and product company producing Elektron 21 (Mg-Nd-Pr-Zr) high-temperature magnesium casting alloy for aerospace applications including helicopter gearbox housings and aerospace structural castings.
